Source: Emerging Markets Q2 2026 Outlook: Shifting Sands
Full Commentary: Aberdeen sees Emerging Asia policy rates near terminal with central banks pausing and only residual final cuts, implying a broadly neutral rate/bond backdrop rather than a fresh easing tailwind. Evidence: In Emerging Asia, policy rates have broadly returned to their pre-pandemic levels and we expect central banks to opt to pause more frequently to observe the impact of delivered monetary easing. Indeed, we believe the region’s central banks are approaching their terminal rates and final rate cuts will be delivered in the Philippines, Indonesia and Thailand in early 2026.. Locator: char:0-8000.

Source: Emerging Markets Q2 2026 Outlook: Shifting Sands
Full Commentary: The house paints a divergent LatAm rate path — cautious/gradual easing in Brazil and Mexico against a potential hike in Chile — netting to a neutral, non-directional stance on the region's bonds despite historically elevated real rates. Evidence: We expect the Banco Central do Brasil (BCB) to begin cautiously easing and Mexico to deliver further cuts. The outlier is Chile, where the central bank may hike before year-end 2026.. Locator: char:0-8000.

AB forecasts oil settling structurally higher post-war on Middle East infrastructure damage, a directional up-forecast on a priced asset. Evidence: But it seems to us that the damage to oil-related infrastructure in the Middle East means that oil prices are likely to settle at least somewhat higher than before the war.. Locator: p.1.

AB's forecast of USD/CNY drifting from 7.10 to 7.20 implies modest yuan depreciation against the dollar, a mildly negative directional call on CNY. Evidence: China — FX Rates vs. USD: 2026F 7.10, 2027F 7.20. Locator: p.5 — China forecast table, 'FX Rates vs. USD'.

AB frames a two-sided ECB path — possible hikes from June on energy-driven inflation, then cuts back to neutral or below from Q2 2027 — which nets to a neutral stance on euro sovereigns, with the 10-Yr yield forecast easing 3.00% to 2.60%. Evidence: That said the probability of a hike clearly increased, and policymakers could start a rate-hiking cycle from June. Depending on the trajectory of energy prices, ECB members who want to preemptively act even without signs of second-round effects might win over those who favor patience. In case the ECB opted to tighten policy rates, the magnitude would be more limited than during the previous cycle when rates were raised by 450 basis points (bps). A cumulated 100 bps of rate hikes would be sufficiently restrictive in a severe scenario. Meanwhile, restrictiveness would have to be withdrawn from the second quarter of 2027, as inflation normalizes and growth is stifled. The ECB would therefore deliver rate cuts back to neutral or potentially below.. Locator: p.7.

AB's base case is BoE cuts resuming from Q4 2026 off restrictive levels with hikes ruled out barring a much larger energy shock, and the 10-Yr gilt forecast easing 4.80% to 4.10% — a modestly supportive tilt, hedged by the contingency on the war ending and near-term extended pause. Evidence: The current macro backdrop calls for an extended pause, but the BoE could resume cuts from the fourth quarter of 2026 if the war ends within a few weeks and energy prices normalize to some extent. The biggest risk to this scenario is the absence of cuts in 2026 rather than hikes. Bank rate is still in restrictive territory, and given the macro backdrop, raising bank rate would require a much larger energy shock.. Locator: p.8.

AB expects the BOJ to resume rate hikes in coming months on firm wage/inflation data, an implied bearish stance on JGBs even though its year-end 10-Yr yield forecast is held flat at 2.25%. Evidence: Wage and inflation data are consistent with our view that the Bank of Japan (BOJ) will raise rates again in the coming months.. Locator: p.8.

AB expects the yen to stay weak near-term pending a fiscal/monetary tightening shift, an implied underweight, though its forecast table shows USD/JPY recovering from 155 (2026F) toward 145 (2027F) as the BOJ tightens. Evidence: In the meantime, we expect the yen to remain weak until the policy framework shifts to include some combination of fiscal and monetary tightening.. Locator: p.8.

AB pairs a cautious EM tilt (easing priced out, stronger USD, currency/financing pressure) with explicit non-conviction — 'the range of potential outcomes remains unusually wide' and acknowledged EM resilience — which nets to a neutral stance on EM fixed income. Evidence: Financial markets are already reflecting this shift in risk perception. Expectations for further monetary easing across EM economies have been largely priced out. At the same time, a stronger US dollar—driven by a classic “risk-off” dynamic—has tightened financial conditions, particularly for more externally vulnerable economies. EM currency depreciation, capital outflows and higher external financing costs would all reinforce downside risks.. Locator: p.9.

With credit spreads narrow versus history, JPM prefers moving up in quality (reinforced p.3 'go up in credit quality... especially across the European banking capital structure'), an overweight to higher-quality issuers. Evidence: We see opportunity to go up in credit quality as spreads generally remain narrow compared with history.. Locator: p.1.

JPM names securitized credit a 'top focus,' citing structural credit enhancement and a resilient U.S. consumer as comforting in stress — an active overweight (also flagged in the In brief, p.1). Evidence: Securitized credit remains a top focus. The inherent credit enhancement of the securitization structure and the resilience of the U.S. consumer are comforting during times of market stress.. Locator: p.3.
